coredata.dc:title: One-step preparation of green tea ash derived and polymer functionalized carbon quantum dots via the thiol-ene click chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: He, Ziyang
Cheng, Jiaxin
Yan, Wenfeng
Long, Wei
Ouyang, Hui
Hu, Xin
Liu, Meiying
Zhou, Naigen
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed a simple, economical and forthright strategy for synthesis of polyethylene glycol functionalized CQDs (PEG@CQDs) through a one-pot thiol-ene click reaction using mercapto polyethylene glycol and green tea ash. These PEG@CQDs displayed excellent waterdispersibility and intense blue-green fluorescence. The fluorescence emission wavelengths of these PEG@CQDs are depended on the excitation wavelengths. Furthermore, cell viability and cell internalization of PEG@CQDs were examined by MTT assay and confocal laser scanning microscopy. The results demonstrated that PEG@CQDs showed low cytotoxicity towards HepG2 cells and strong green fluorescence could be detected after HepG2 cells were excited with a 405 nm laser. All the above results demonstrated that PEG@CQDs possess small size, high water dispersibility and fluorescence properties and could be potentially utilized for cell fluorescent imaging. We hope the method will provide some novel ideas for preparation of functional CQDs and inspired the biomedical applications of these functional CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiol-ene click chemistry
Functionalized carbon quantum dots
Green tea ash

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation and confocal microscopic imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The 1H NMR spectra of mPEG and PEG-SH in DMSO-D6, the insets showed the partial enlarged detail. (B) The 1H NMR spectrum of PEG@CQDs in DMSO-D6, the inset showed the partial enlarged spectrum. (C) The FT-IR spectra of tea ash and PEG@CQDs. (D) TGA curves of Tea ash, PEG-SH, and PEG@CQDs, the temperature ranged from room temperature to 700 °C.

(A) TEM image of PEG@CQDs, scale bar = 0.5 μm, and (B) the enlarged TEM image of the red circle area at Fig. 2 A, scale bar = 100 nm, (C) hydrodynamic size distribution of PEG@CQDs based on DLS measurement, (D) XPS survey spectra of tea ash and PEG@CQDs with binding energy from 0 to 1400 eV. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

XPS spectra of PEG@CQDs, (A) C 1s region, (B) O 1s region, (C) N 1s region, (D) S 2p region. The existence of CC should be attributed to the formation of a conjugation system during the ignition of tea ash and it is the reason for the generation of fluorescence. A high percentage of CO was found in the C 1s spectrum, further indicating that PEG@CQDs are majorly composited with PEG.

(A) Normalized emission spectra for PEG@CQDs aqueous solution excited by indicated wavelengths; (B) Excitation and emission spectra of PEG@CQDs in aqueous solutions and their photographs at daylight (cuvette a) and 365 nm laser (cuvette b). (C) The normalized fluorescence intensity of PEG@CQDs aqueous solution with different irradiation times by UV lamp (365 nm) for 0.5, 1, and 2 h. The inset shows the corresponding emission spectra; (D) The normalized fluorescence intensity of PEG@CQDs aqueous solution with different pH values, and the inset shows the corresponding emission spectra.

(A) Cell viability of PEG@CQDs based on MTT assay. HepG2 cells were cultivated with different concentrations of PEG@CQDs up to 160 µg mL−1 for 24 h. CLSM images of HepG2 cells after incubation with PEG@CQDs for 4 h. (B) bright field images, (C) images of cells excited with a 405 nm laser, and (D) their overlapping images. Scale bar = 25 μm.
Schematic showing the preparation of fluorescent PEG@CQDs through thiol-ene click reaction using tea ash as carbon source and thiol group terminated PEG (PEG-SH) as the reagents.

head.ce:title: One-step preparation of green tea ash derived and polymer functionalized carbon quantum dots via the thiol-ene click chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a simple, economical and forthright strategy for synthesis of polyethylene glycol functionalized CQDs (PEG@CQDs) through a one-pot thiol-ene click reaction using mercapto polyethylene glycol and green tea ash. These PEG@CQDs displayed excellent waterdispersibility and intense blue-green fluorescence. The fluorescence emission wavelengths of these PEG@CQDs are depended on the excitation wavelengths. Furthermore, cell viability and cell internalization of PEG@CQDs were examined by MTT assay and confocal laser scanning microscopy. The results demonstrated that PEG@CQDs showed low cytotoxicity towards HepG2 cells and strong green fluorescence could be detected after HepG2 cells were excited with a 405 nm laser. All the above results demonstrated that PEG@CQDs possess small size, high water dispersibility and fluorescence properties and could be potentially utilized for cell fluorescent imaging. We hope the method will provide some novel ideas for preparation of functional CQDs and inspired the biomedical applications of these functional CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) have huge potential in applications ranging from disease diagnosis to energy utilization owing to their unique properties bring from quantum size effect, surface effect, dielectric confinement effect, coulomb blocking effect and quantum tunneling effect [1–7]. Compared with traditional organic fluorescent molecules, QDs possess advantages such as longer fluorescence lifetime, broadband excitation spectra, and tunable emission band [8–14]. The conventional semiconductor QDs (SQDs) are composed of II–VI elements (such as CdSe, ZnS, or CdS) or III–V elements (such as GaP, InP, or InAs) that show outstanding photophysical properties and their great potential applications in light-emitting diodes biosensors, biological imaging, and sensors and diagnostics have already been extensively reported [15–25]. However, SQDs are derived from heavy metal precursors, that often release toxic metal ions during applied in vitro and in vivo. Therefore, the potential toxicity of SQDs for biomedical applications have been carefully examined over the past few decades [26,27]. For example, Lin’s group evaluated the toxicity of a cadmium/selenium/tellurium (Cd/Se/Te)-based QDs in kidneys of mice. They found that free Cd indeed can be released from the Cd/Se/Te-based QDs and increase the risk of renal toxicity [28]. Yan et al reported the potential vascular endothelial toxicity of Cd/Te QDs in vitro. They demonstrated that Cd/Te QDs (0.1–100 μg/mL) can decrease the cell viability of human umbilical vein ECs via activation of mitochondrial death and induction of endothelial apoptosis [29]. Therefore, preparation of QDs absent toxic metal ions to be important and meaningful. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been attracted much attention and interest for biomedical applications, that have been regarded as a powerful competitor of SQDs owing to their outstanding optical properties and better biocompatibility [30–32]. The first discovery of CQDs happened in 2004 by Xu et al during the single-walled carbon nanotubes purification [33,34]. CQDs are defined as carbon nanoparticles with a size of 2 to 20 nm and with the shape of spherical or ellipsoid. CQDs also possess advantages such as high chemical stability, broadband excitation spectra, tunable emission band, and long fluorescent lifetime. Unlike the high potential cytotoxicity of SQDs, it has been confirmed that CQDs are environmental friendliness and of low cytotoxicity, which makes them a prominent advantage in the field of biomedicine. The mechanism of CQDs associated with photoluminescence is not entirely clear; it might be the radiative recombination of the energy-trapping sites on the surface of CQDs [35].

Though CQDs have exhibited some unique performance, the stable and simple preparation of CQDs from a cheap and accessible source of carbon is still a challenge. Up to now, various preparation methods of CQDs have been developed such as laser ablation method [36–38], electrochemical synthesis [39,40], hydrothermal method [41,42] and hydrothermal treatment of different carbon sources [43,44]. These CQDs prepared by the above methods all possess good water solubility, nanometer size, and multi-color. However, these preparation routes mentioned above suffer from more or fewer limitations such as complex processes, expensive equipment, rigorous synthetic conditions, and high cost. Therefore, chemical products prepared from cheap, easily available, renewable resources instead of conventional chemicals attracted increasing attention and research interesting [45]. For example, Ding’s group prepared a non-toxic, low-cost, and eco-friendly CQDs by heating an ethanol solution of pulp-free lemon juice at 190 °C for 10 h [31]. Wang et al demonstrated that CQDs can be obtained by heating milk for 2 h at 180 °C [30]. Yang’s group synthesized CQDs via the hydrothermal treatment of glucose with monopotassium phosphate [46]. Although these methods can prepare nontoxic CQDs from cheap, renewable sources of carbon, these approaches lack designability and maneuverability [47,48]. Therefore, to endow CQDs with other novel properties and functions, further surface modification is often required. To the best of our knowledge, direct synthesis of polymers functionalized CQDs has been rarely reported.

In this work, we report a simple, economical, one-step strategy for the preparation of PEGylated CQDs (PEG@CQDs) by using green tea ash as a renewable carbon source and mercaptopolyethylene glycol as the surface functional coating (see Scheme 1 ). The obtained PEG@CQDs show great water solubility and strong photoluminescence. The PEG@CQDs can be used as fluorescent probes for biological imaging. More importantly, this approach reported in this work can also be extended for the preparation of diverse functionalized CQDs via choosing the mercapto compounds or mercapto polymers and abundant carbon sources.

The following materials were used. Methoxypolyethylene glycol (mPEG, average Mw: 1900 Da), N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC, Mw: 206.33 Da, 99.0%), 4-Dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP, Mw: 122.17 Da, 99%), thioglycolic acid (Mw: 92.12, ≥98%), dicumyl peroxyide (Mw: 270.37 Da, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, Mw: 73.09 Da, AR) and ethanol (Mw: 46.07 Da, AR) were obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.. All chemical agents were used as received without further purification. The samples were characterized by several techniques to examine their size, morphology, chemical composition, and optical properties. The detailed information for the characterization of these samples was listed in the supporting information.

The preparation of mercapto polyethylene glycol (PEG-SH) was implemented by dehydration between hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. In brief, mPEG (2.0 g), thioglycolic acid (120 mg), DCC (250 mg) and DMAP (50 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL dry toluene. The reaction was kept at 70 °C with vigorous stirring and a nitrogen atmosphere for 12 h. The obtained liquid was encapsulated into a dialysis bag (with a molecular weight cut off 1000 Da) and soaked in frequently updated water and ethanol for 24 h each to remove impurities. After purification, the liquid was placed in a vacuum drying oven for 48 h to obtain a yellow–brown solid (PEG-SH).

The abandoned tea leaves were dried at 70 °C for 12 h and then the solid was mixed with 10 mL absolute alcohol and ignited to get gray-black powder. The gray-black powders (1 g) were dispersed in anhydrous DMF with ultrasonic treatment for 3 min and then PEG-SH (2 g) and dicumyl peroxyide (1 g) was added. The reaction bulb was kept full of nitrogen and stirred at 170 °C for 24 h under reflux. After natural cooling, the brown transparent liquid was separated by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 3 min and then the brown transparent liquid was encapsulated into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut off of 7000 Da and soaked in water and ethanol for 24 h to remove the impurities. The final product was obtained by freeze drying for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation and confocal microscopic imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Biocompatibility is a vital parameter for fluorescent nanomaterials in biological applications. In this work, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was adopted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of PEG@CQDs according to our previous work [49–52]. The detailed experimental procedures for cell viability evaluation were shown in supporting information. The cell uptake behavior of PEG@CQDs by HepG2 cells was evaluated by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The cell images were taken with a CLSM (Zeiss 710 3-channel, Zeiss, Germany) at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. Detailed information was displayed in the supporting information.

As the world's top three non-alcoholic beverages, global tea production exceeded 6 million tons in 2019. A number of bioactive constituents, such as tea polyphenol, caffeine, lipopolysaccharide and theanine have been found in green tea. These bioactive compounds possess various physiological and pharmacological effects and different health benefits, including antioxidant, lowering blood lipid, anti-tumor and anti-aging et al [53]. Compared with other expensive carbon source, the easily available and cheap tea ash could be an economical carbon source for mass preparation of fluorescent CQDs. By adopting the thiol-ene click reaction method, fluorescent CQDs can be designed on purpose. It provided a novel approach for preparing functional fluorescent CQDs. The PEGylated CQDs are composed of the core of CQDs with a PEG shell. The successful introduction of PEG on the surface of CQDs through a one-pot thiol-ene click chemistry was confirmed by the following characterization techniques. 1H NMR spectra were utilized to characterize the chemical compositions of mPEG, PEG-SH, and PEG@CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1
A, the hydrogen peaks of different chemical environments on PEG and PEG-SH have been identified by letters and their corresponding signals have been marked. As compared with mPEG, all hydrogens of mPEG were also found in PEG-SH. However, two weak signals appeared at 4.55 ppm and 1.25 ppm, that could be attributed to the COOCH 2 (“e”) and SH (“g”), respectively. Based on the results of 1H NMR spectra, we could conclude that the thiol group has been conjugated onto mPEG through esterification. Moreover, based on the 1H NMR spectrum of PEG-SH, the replacement ratio of mPEG by SH is about 57.2%. The 1H NMR spectrum of PEG@CQDs was displayed in Fig. 1 B. It can be seen that a typical chemical shift at 3.5 ppm was detected. It could be attributed to the CH 2 of PEG chains, the chemical shift at 3.3 ppm could be ascribed to H2O and the solvent signal appears at 2.5 ppm. Besides, the signal of CO OCH2 (“e”) could also be found in the same chemical shift compared with Fig. 1 A. The above results indicated that PEG-SH was indeed introduced on the surface of CQDs and the successful formation of PEG functionalized CQDs.

FT-IR spectra were further used to characterize the surface functional groups and chemical structure of PEG@CQDs. The FT-IR spectra of tea ash, mPEG, PEG-SH and PEG@CQDs were determined and compared. As shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. 1 C, the infrared spectra of mPEG, PEG-SH and PEG@CQDs are very similar because they both contain large amounts of PEG. Some common characteristic signals were found in the spectra of mPEG and PEG-SH. These peaks could be assigned below: stretching vibration of OH (3500 cm−1), stretching vibration of CH2 (2855 cm−1), shear deformation vibration or in-plane bending vibration of CH2 (1200–1500 cm−1), and stretching vibration of CO (1090 cm−1) [54]. It should be noticed that a weak signal at 1653 cm−1 was found in the FT-IR spectrum of PEG-SH. It could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of OCO. However, the signal of carbonyl group is relatively weak, it is possibly ascribed to intense characteristic signals of mPEG. The above comparison could also be implied that successful synthesis of PEG-SH by dehydration between hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The FT-IR spectrum of tea ash was displayed in Fig. 1 C. Five strong signals were appeared at 1688, 1604, 1024, 748 and 561 cm−1 on the FT-IR spectra of tea ash. They could be respectively ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO (1688 cm−1); stretching vibration of CC or Ar (1604 cm−1); stretching vibration of CO (1024 cm−1) from ethers; out-of-plane bending vibration of CCH or ArH (748 cm−1 and 561 cm−1). More importantly, as compared with FT-IR spectrum of PEG-SH, a stronger peak at 1669 cm−1 was found in the spectrum of PEG@CQDs. This peak is much close to the stretching vibration of CO (1688 cm−1) of tea ash, and the intensity is much stronger than the stretching vibration of OCO of PEG-SH. It suggested that the PEG@CQDs are composited with CQDs from tea ash and PEG-SH. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks (such as CH2 at 2855 cm−1 and CO at 1090 cm−1) from PEG were also observed in PEG@CQDs. This further demonstrated that we have successfully prepared PEG@CQDs through the one-pot thiol-ene click reaction.

The thermal stability of samples was analyzed by TGA. The TGA curves of tea ash, PEG-SH, and PEG@CQDs were shown in Fig. 1 D. It's clear that the weight of tea ash continuously decreased with increasing temperature, which could be ascribed to the volatilization of water and volatile organic matter, degradation of macromolecules such as fiber and vegetable proteins. The weight loss of tea ash could arrive at about 40% at 700 °C. The TGA curve of PEG@CQDs is very similar to the curve of PEG-SH. The major weight loss of PEG-SH and PEG@CQDs occurred at the temperature between 350 °C and 450 °C. Reversely, an obvious difference from the curve of tea ash could be observed that the major weight loss of PEG-SH occurred at temperatures interval of 350 °C to 450 °C and the weight loss ratio is over 95%, which illustrated that PEG chains would be completely decomposed in the temperature range from 350 °C to 450 °C. Meanwhile, the weight loss of PEG@CQDs showed a very similar tendency to PEG-SH. Therefore, we can conclude that PEG@CQDs approximately consist of more than 95% PEG polymer and less than 5% CQDs in terms of TGA curves.

Because CQDs were deeply buried in polyethylene glycol pellets, some characterization methods often fail to directly assess the successful preparation of CQDs. Hence, a transmission electron microscope (TEM) was employed to examine the size and morphology of PEG@CQDs. As displayed in Fig. 2 , several elliptical shadows of uneven size can be observed in Fig. 2 A, which could be judged as PEG particles. Meanwhile, the enlarged image of the red circle area was shown in Fig. 2 B, we can find some black dots with size about 10 nm that can be regarded as CQDs from green ash. Therefore, the above results can demonstrate that CQDs are wrapped in PEG particles and it also provides strong evidence for the successful preparation of PEGylated CQDs. The size of PEG@CQDs showed in the image tends to be larger than in the solution, because the macromolecular chains are close to each other as the solvent evaporates in the sample preparation process, resulting in them tend to entangle themselves to form larger particles by the van der Waals forces. Therefore, in order to accurately judge the size of PEG@CQDs within applications, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique was adopted to measure their hydrodynamic size in water solution. The result of DLS was shown in Fig. 2 C, it is observed that the particle size of PEG@CQDs is mainly distributed at 200 ± 100 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.224, and the size is suitable for application in biological applications. The semi-quantitative analysis of surface elements of tea ash and PEG@CQDs was relied on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique and the full XPS spectra of tea ash and PEG@CQDs were displayed in Fig. 2 D. It is evident that the tea ash and PEG@CQDs are mainly composed of C and O with some other elements such as N and S. The C and O should be attributed to the biological macromolecules while N and S are possibly originated from organic molecules of green tea ash.

Besides, Table S1 shows the element content (%) of tea ash and PEG@CQDs calculated based on XPS analysis. Respectively, tea ash mainly contains C (74.55%), O (20.59%), N (4.67%), and S (0.19%); PEG@CQDs mainly contains C (67.91%), O (31.03%), N (0.95%), and S (0.11%). Based on the elemental content of tea ash and PEG@CQDs, we can find that element percentages of C, N, and S were decreased to some extent in PEG@CQDs as compared with tea ash. This is possibly ascribed to the absence of N and relatively low percentages of C and S in PEG-SH. On the other hand, the content of O was increased from 20.59% to 31.03% after tea ash was transformed into PEG@CQDs. It can be attributed to the high percentage of O in PEG-SH. Moreover, the carbon–oxygen ratio (67.91:31.03) of PEG@CQDs is close to that of mPEG (2:1), which further proves the obtained CQDs were packaged by PEG chains. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of PEG@CQDs is shown in Fig. 3
A and the spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks at around 284.9 eV, 285.8 eV, and 287.0 eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CO, and CO, respectively. The existence of CC implied that the formation of conjugation system during tea ash was ignited and it should be the reason for generation fluorescence. The high percentage of CO should be derived from the PEG-SH and suggested PEG@CQDs are majorly composited with PEG-SH. Moreover, CO with considerable intensity was also found in PEG@CQDs. It should be originated from tea ash during incineration. In the high-resolution O 1s spectrum, the spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at around 532.5 eV and 533.2 eV, which are attributed to CO and CO, respectively. The signal of carbonyl may be derived from tea ash nanoparticles. The high-resolution N 1s and S 2p XPS spectra were shown in Fig. 3 C and Fig. 3 D. The element N should come from tea ash owing to the absence of N in PEG@SH. Element S should originate from both tea ash and PEG-SH. All the above results confirm the successful synthesis of PEG functionalized CQDs through the one-pot thiol-ene click reaction.

Based on all the above characterization results, we can conclude that the PEGylated CQDs have been successful prepared by a thiol-ene click reaction with tea ash as the carbon source. Therefore, in the following sections, optical properties of PEG@CQDs were investigated to evaluate their potential in fluorescent imaging applications. Fig. 4
A and Fig. S2 revealed the multiple emission of PEG@CQDs by adjusting the excitation wavelengths with 20 nm increment and the normalized fluorescence intensity varies with different excitation wavelengths has been displayed in Fig. 4 A. The fluorescence intensity is strong at excitation wavelengths between 320 nm and 400 nm and it is obvious that the emission wavelengths of PEG@CQDs get a redshift with increasing of excitation wavelengths. Fig. S2 shows their fluorescence intensity first increases from 300 to 340 nm and then decreases as the excitation wavelengths increase from 340 to 500 nm. This unique phenomenon is also found in other CQDs by other’s reports and it is possibly attributed to the heterogeneity of CQDs size (quantum effect) and different emissive trap sites on the surface [55,56]. As we can observe in Fig. 4 A, the emission wavelengths of PEG@CQDs were ranged from 350 nm to 650 nm, which means optional multi-colorful fluorescent nanoparticles can be achieved by requirement via adjusting exciting wavelengths. Meanwhile, Fig. 4 B displays the strongest emission at 433 nm (blue curve) and its corresponding excitation at 337 nm (red curve). The mark “Q” could be observed through the transparent “cuvette a” with PEG@CQDs aqueous solution, which can demonstrate the excellent water solubility of PEG@CQDs. The luminous “cuvette b” irradiated by 365 nm laser also confirms their photoluminescence. The UV–Vis spectrum of PEG@CQDs in aqueous solution was displayed in Fig. S3 , there are two main absorption signals at 208 nm and 278 nm, which could respectively be attributed to π → π* transition of aromatic rings and n → π* transition of CO.

Photostability is an important parameter of photoluminescent nanomaterials for potential bioimaging applications. Fig. 4 C shows that the normalized fluorescence intensity of PEG@CQDs aqueous solution with different irradiation times. We examine their fluorescence intensity after irradiation (365 nm) with different time intervals (0, 0.5, 1 and 2 h). The fluorescence intensity PEG@CQDs can still be maintained at more than 85% after irradiation for 2 h compared with initial fluorescence intensity, and its corresponding emission spectra were shown in the inset. The above results indicate that PEG@CQDs possess good photostability, which guarantees their effective applications in biomedical fields, especially for long-term fluorescent imaging. Fig. S4 shows the fluorescence lifetime decay curve of PEG@CQDs, meanwhile, according to the related fitting data (Table. S1) and formula of calculating the average life span, the calculated average lifetime is 5.15 ns. Accordingly, the fluorescence quantum yield of PEG@CQDs was determined to be about 3%. Furthermore, the pH sensibility of PEG@CQDs has also been evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 D, the normalized fluorescence intensity of PEG@CQDs aqueous solution with different pH values demonstrated the influence of pH values for luminous efficiency. It is clear that in the pH range of 3.1–9.3, the fluorescence intensity of PEG@CQDs is gradually increased to a maximum value at pH 9.3, while in the pH range of 9.3–12.2, the fluorescence intensity was gradually decreased and has a minimum intensity at pH 12.2. Also, the minimum fluorescence intensity still can reach 72.3% as compared with the maximum intensity. The above results illustrated PEG@CQDs can still maintain high fluorescence signals under a wide range of pH and PEG@CQDs derived from tea ash possess good acid-base tolerance.

All the above characterization results demonstrated that PEG@CQDs possess high water dispersibility, small size and tunable fluorescence and excellent photostability. In the following section, the cell viability of PEG@CQDs was determined by a typical MTT assay to evaluate their application potential in biomedical fields. As shown in Fig. 5
A, HepG2 cells were cultured and maintained with 0–160 µg mL−1 PEG@CQDs for 24 h. The results show that HepG2 cells still can maintain high cell viability after cultured with PEG@CQDs, even cultured with 160 µg mL−1 for 24 h. At the highest concentrations, the cell viability value of PEG@CQDs is still over 90%. Therefore, the obtained PEG@CQDs derived from tea ash show no significant cytotoxicity for HepG2 cells, which implies that PEG@CQDs are promising candidates for biomedical applications. The performance of PEG@CQDs applied in fluorescent cell imaging was evaluated by examination of cell uptake behavior of PEG@CQDs. The cell imaging of HepG2 cells was examined using CLSM with a laser with excitation wavelength of 405 nm. Fig. 5 B shows the bright field of HepG2 cells after cells were treated with PEG@CQDs for 4 h. It can be seen that HepG2 cells still can keep normal morphology and size under natural light after incubation with the PEG@CQDs for 4 h, further evidencing low cytotoxicity of PEG@CQDs. Fig. 5 C is fluorescent imaging of HepG2 cells with an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. The results from cell imaging confirmed that agglomeration of green-emissive PEG@CQDs could be internalized by cells and distributed inside the cells. After merging images of bright field and fluorescent imaging, we can find that PEG@CQDs were mainly distributed in cytoplasm with absence of fluorescence signals at locations of cell nuclei. Therefore, we can conclude that PEG@CQDs can be internalized by HepG2 cells and cells still maintain their normal morphology based on CLSM images. These results imply that PEG@CQDs possess well cytocompatibility and desirable fluorescence properties for fluorescence imaging applications. Moreover, as indicated by fluorescence spectra, we can speculate that PEG@CQDs also can be used for multi-color imaging owing to their tunable and excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission.

In summary, we reported a novel strategy for preparation of PEG functionalized CQDs through a one-pot thiol-ene click reaction using tea ash as the carbon source and thiol group terminated PEG as the polymer shell. Compared with other common methods for preparing CQDs, this approach exhibits some obvious advantages, such as simplicity for experimental operation, high effectiveness and well designability of functional polymers and other thiol-containing molecules. Besides, carbon source was derived from tea ash, which is a cheap, and renewable environment-friendly material. More importantly, this approach can be achieved between extensive mercapto compounds or mercapto polymers and abundant carbon sources to prepare diverse functionalized CQDs. The results from characterization and biological assays confirmed that these PEG functionalized CQDs possess small size, well water dispersibility, tunable fluorescence and low toxicity. These outstanding properties make PEG@CQDs promising for biomedical applications. Finally, we expect that this study can be an alternative strategy for the design and fabrication of excellent fluorescent multi-functional CQDs with great potential for various applications.

Ziyang He: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Jiaxin Cheng: Investigation, Methodology. Wen feng Yan: Investigation, Methodology. Wei Long: Investigation, Methodology. Hui Ouyang: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation. Xin Hu: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation. Meiying Liu: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation. Naigen Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xiaoyong Zhang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yen Wei: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21788102, 21865016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2021.108743.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots for live cells, vivo imaging, and diagnostics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Michalet, F. Pinaud, L.A. Bentolila, J.M. Tsay, S. Doose, J.J. Li, G. Sundaresan, A.M. Wu, S.S. Gambhir, S. Weiss, Quantum Dots for Live Cells, in Vivo Imaging, and Diagnostics, Science, 307 (2005) 538-544.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.O. Dabbousi, J. Rodriguezviejo, F.V. Mikulec, J.R. Heine, H. Mattoussi, R. Ober, K.F.J. And, M.G. Bawendi, (CdSe)ZnS Core−Shell Quantum Dots: Synthesis and Characterization of a Size Series of Highly Luminescent Nanocrystallites, J. Mater. Chem. B, 101 (1997) 9463-9475.
